Part I - Ford GoBike System Data Exploration¶

by Massimiliano Sermi¶

Introduction¶

The dataset contains individual bike trips from the Ford GoBike bike-sharing system in the San Francisco Bay Area during February 2019. Each record represents one bike ride and includes information such as trip duration, start and end time, station information, bike ID, user type, member gender, member birth year, and whether the user participated in the Bike Share for All program.

The goal of this exploration is to understand how different users interact with the bike-sharing system, especially how ride duration and ride frequency vary by time, user type, gender, and age

Preliminary Wrangling¶

In [1]:
# import all packages and set plots to be embedded inline
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

%matplotlib inline

# set default plot style
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")

df = pd.read_csv('201902-fordgobike-tripdata (1).csv')

The dataset contains missing values primarily in the member_birth_year and member_gender variables. These missing values were retained for most analyses, while age-based analyses were performed only on records with valid birth-year information.

In [7]:
# create a copy for cleaning and feature engineering
bike = df.copy()
In [8]:
# convert start and end time to datetime
bike['start_time'] = pd.to_datetime(bike['start_time'])
bike['end_time'] = pd.to_datetime(bike['end_time'])

# create duration in minutes
bike['duration_min'] = bike['duration_sec'] / 60

# create time-based variables
bike['start_hour'] = bike['start_time'].dt.hour
bike['start_day'] = bike['start_time'].dt.day_name()

# create age variable
bike['member_age'] = 2019 - bike['member_birth_year']
In [9]:
# order weekdays correctly
weekday_order = ['Monday', 'Tuesday', 'Wednesday', 'Thursday', 'Friday', 'Saturday', 'Sunday']

bike['start_day'] = pd.Categorical(
    bike['start_day'],
    categories=weekday_order,
    ordered=True
)
In [10]:
# remove extreme or unrealistic ages for clearer analysis
bike_clean = bike.query('member_age <= 80 or member_age != member_age').copy()
In [11]:
# check cleaned dataset
bike_clean.shape
Out[11]:
(183220, 20)

What is the structure of your dataset?¶

The original dataset contains 183,412 bike rides and 16 variables. The variables include trip duration, start and end timestamps, start and end station information, bike ID, user type, member birth year, member gender, and participation in the Bike Share for All program.

After preliminary cleaning and feature engineering, additional variables were created, including trip duration in minutes, start hour, start day, and member age.

What is/are the main feature(s) of interest in your dataset?¶

The main features of interest are ride duration and ride frequency. I want to understand how long rides typically last, when rides happen most often, and whether usage patterns differ between Subscribers and Customers.

What features in the dataset do you think will help support your investigation into your feature(s) of interest?¶

The most useful supporting features are:

  • user_type
  • member_gender
  • member_age
  • start_hour
  • start_day
  • bike_share_for_all_trip

These features help explain differences in ride behavior across user groups and time periods.

Univariate Exploration¶

Question 1: What is the distribution of ride duration?¶

In [12]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
bins = np.arange(0, bike_clean['duration_min'].max() + 1, 1)

plt.hist(data=bike_clean, x='duration_min', bins=bins)
plt.xlim(0, 60)
plt.xlabel('Ride Duration (minutes)')
plt.ylabel('Number of Rides')
plt.title('Distribution of Ride Duration')
plt.show()

Observation¶

Ride duration is strongly right-skewed. Most rides are short and last under 20 minutes. A small number of rides last much longer, which creates a long tail in the distribution.

Question 2: What does ride duration look like on a log scale?¶

In [13]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))

bins = 10 ** np.arange(
    np.log10(bike_clean['duration_min'].min()),
    np.log10(bike_clean['duration_min'].max()) + 0.05,
    0.05
)

plt.hist(data=bike_clean, x='duration_min', bins=bins)
plt.xscale('log')
plt.xlabel('Ride Duration (minutes, log scale)')
plt.ylabel('Number of Rides')
plt.title('Log-Scaled Distribution of Ride Duration')
plt.show()

Observation¶

The log transformation makes the distribution easier to interpret. Most rides are concentrated around short durations, especially between approximately 5 and 15 minutes.

Question 3: Which user type is most common?¶

In [14]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5))

ax = sns.countplot(data=bike_clean, x='user_type')

total = len(bike_clean)

for patch in ax.patches:
    count = patch.get_height()
    percentage = 100 * count / total
    ax.text(
        patch.get_x() + patch.get_width() / 2,
        count,
        f'{percentage:.1f}%',
        ha='center',
        va='bottom'
    )

plt.xlabel('User Type')
plt.ylabel('Number of Rides')
plt.title('Number of Rides by User Type')
plt.show()
In [15]:
bike_clean['user_type'].value_counts(normalize=True) * 100
Out[15]:
user_type
Subscriber    89.161118
Customer      10.838882
Name: proportion, dtype: float64

Observation¶

Subscribers account for approximately 89% of rides, while Customers account for about 11%. This shows that the Ford GoBike system was used primarily by regular members during February 2019.

Question 4: On which days are rides most frequent?¶

In [16]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))

sns.countplot(data=bike_clean, x='start_day', order=weekday_order)

plt.xlabel('Day of Week')
plt.ylabel('Number of Rides')
plt.title('Number of Rides by Day of Week')
plt.xticks(rotation=30)
plt.show()

Observation¶

Rides are more frequent during weekdays than weekends. This suggests that many users may be using the bikes for commuting or regular weekday travel.

Discuss the distribution(s) of your variable(s) of interest. Were there any unusual points? Did you need to perform any transformations?¶

Ride duration has a highly right-skewed distribution, with most rides being short and a few rides lasting much longer. Because of this skew, I used a log scale to better understand the main concentration of trip durations. I also limited some plots to shorter ride durations to avoid long outliers making the visualizations difficult to read.

Of the features you investigated, were there any unusual distributions? Did you perform any operations on the data to tidy, adjust, or change the form of the data? If so, why did you do this?¶

The age variable had some unusually high values, probably caused by incorrect or outdated birth-year entries. I created a cleaned dataset by excluding members older than 80 for age-related analysis. I also converted start and end times into datetime format and created new variables such as ride duration in minutes, start hour, start day, and member age to make the analysis easier.

Bivariate Exploration¶

Question 5: How does ride duration vary by user type?¶

In [17]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))

sns.boxplot(data=bike_clean, x='user_type', y='duration_min')

plt.ylim(0, 60)
plt.xlabel('User Type')
plt.ylabel('Ride Duration (minutes)')
plt.title('Ride Duration by User Type')
plt.show()

Observation¶

Customers generally take longer rides than Subscribers. Subscribers appear to use the service for shorter and more regular trips.

Question 6: Is there a relationship between age and ride duration?¶

In [18]:
sample = bike_clean.dropna(subset=['member_age']).sample(3000, random_state=42)

plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5))

sns.scatterplot(data=sample, x='member_age', y='duration_min', alpha=0.4)

plt.ylim(0, 80)
plt.xlabel('Member Age')
plt.ylabel('Ride Duration (minutes)')
plt.title('Ride Duration by Member Age')
plt.show()

Observation¶

The scatterplot does not reveal a strong linear relationship between age and ride duration. The scatterplot does not reveal a strong linear relationship between age and ride duration. Ride lengths remain relatively consistent across age groups, with most rides remaining relatively short regardless of rider age.

Question 7: When are bikes used most during the week?¶

In [19]:
heat_data = bike_clean.groupby(
    ['start_day', 'start_hour'],
    observed=False
).size().reset_index(name='ride_count')

heat_pivot = heat_data.pivot(index='start_day', columns='start_hour', values='ride_count')

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6))

sns.heatmap(heat_pivot, cmap='viridis')

plt.xlabel('Hour of Day')
plt.ylabel('Day of Week')
plt.title('Bike Ride Frequency by Day and Hour')
plt.show()

Observation¶

The heatmap shows strong ride activity during weekday commuting hours, especially around 8 AM and 5 PM. Weekend usage is lower and more spread across the day.

Question 8: How does ride frequency differ by gender?¶

In [20]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))

sns.countplot(data=bike_clean, x='member_gender', order=['Male', 'Female', 'Other'])

plt.xlabel('Member Gender')
plt.ylabel('Number of Rides')
plt.title('Number of Rides by Gender')
plt.show()

Observation¶

Male members account for the largest number of rides in the dataset. Female and Other gender categories have fewer rides.

Talk about some of the relationships you observed in this part of the investigation. How did the feature(s) of interest vary with other features in the dataset?¶

Ride duration varies clearly by user type. Customers tend to take longer trips, while Subscribers tend to take shorter trips. Ride frequency also varies strongly by day and hour, with weekday commuting peaks around the morning and evening.

Did you observe any interesting relationships between the other features (not the main feature(s) of interest)?¶

The relationship between day of week and hour of day is especially interesting. Weekday rides are concentrated around commute times, while weekend rides are more evenly distributed throughout the day. This suggests different usage purposes between weekday and weekend riders.

Multivariate Exploration¶

Question 9: Do hourly riding patterns differ between Subscribers and Customers?¶

In [21]:
hourly = bike_clean.groupby(
    ['user_type','start_hour']
).size().reset_index(name='count')

g = sns.FacetGrid(
    hourly,
    col='user_type',
    height=4,
    aspect=1.4
)

g.map_dataframe(
    sns.barplot,
    x='start_hour',
    y='count',
    color='steelblue'
)

g.set_axis_labels('Hour of Day', 'Number of Rides')
g.set_titles('{col_name}')
g.fig.suptitle('Hourly Ride Patterns by User Type', y=1.05)

plt.show()

Observation¶

Subscribers show clear peaks during commute hours, especially around 8 AM and 5 PM. Customers have a flatter distribution, suggesting more casual or recreational use.

Question 10: How do user type and gender together relate to ride duration?¶

In [22]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

sns.boxplot(
    data=bike_clean,
    x='user_type',
    y='duration_min',
    hue='member_gender'
)

plt.ylim(0, 60)
plt.xlabel('User Type')
plt.ylabel('Ride Duration (minutes)')
plt.title('Ride Duration by User Type and Gender')
plt.legend(title='Gender')
plt.show()

Observation¶

Customers generally have longer rides than Subscribers across gender groups. User type appears to explain ride-duration differences more strongly than gender.

Question 11: What is the relationship between age, duration, and user type?¶

In [23]:
sample = bike_clean.dropna(subset=['member_age']).sample(4000, random_state=42)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))

sns.scatterplot(
    data=sample,
    x='member_age',
    y='duration_min',
    hue='user_type',
    alpha=0.5
)

plt.ylim(0, 80)
plt.xlabel('Member Age')
plt.ylabel('Ride Duration (minutes)')
plt.title('Ride Duration by Age and User Type')
plt.legend(title='User Type')
plt.show()

Observation¶

Most rides are short across all age groups. Customers are more likely to have longer rides than Subscribers, even when age is considered.

Question 12: How do ride duration patterns vary by user type across weekdays?¶

In [24]:
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

sns.pointplot(
    data=bike_clean,
    x='start_day',
    y='duration_min',
    hue='user_type',
    order=weekday_order,
    errorbar=None
)

plt.xlabel('Day of Week')
plt.ylabel('Average Ride Duration (minutes)')
plt.title('Average Ride Duration by Day and User Type')
plt.xticks(rotation=30)
plt.legend(title='User Type')
plt.show()

Observation¶

Customers have longer average ride durations than Subscribers on every day of the week. Average ride duration tends to be higher on weekends, especially for Customers.

Talk about some of the relationships you observed in this part of the investigation. Were there features that strengthened each other in terms of looking at your feature(s) of interest?¶

User type and time-based features strengthened the analysis. Subscribers show commute-like behavior, with many rides during weekday morning and evening peaks. Customers show longer ride durations and less concentrated hourly usage, suggesting more casual or recreational use.

Were there any interesting or surprising interactions between features?¶

One interesting interaction is that Customers ride longer than Subscribers across multiple categories, including gender and day of week. This suggests that user type is one of the strongest indicators of ride behavior in this dataset.

Conclusions¶

The main findings from the exploratory analysis are:

  • Most Ford GoBike rides are short, usually under 20 minutes.
  • Ride duration is strongly right-skewed, so a log transformation helps reveal the main distribution more clearly.
  • Subscribers make up the majority of rides.
  • Customers generally take longer rides than Subscribers.
  • Weekday ride frequency peaks during commute hours, especially around 8 AM and 5 PM.
  • Weekend rides are less concentrated by hour and appear more recreational.
  • Gender differences exist in ride counts, but user type appears more important for explaining ride duration.
  • Age does not show a strong visible relationship with ride duration.

Overall, the analysis suggests that Subscribers mostly use the Ford GoBike system for short, regular, commute-like trips, while Customers tend to use the service for longer and more casual rides.
